skill.name: turkce-kod
skill.description: Türkçe yazılım projeleri için kod dokümantasyonu, yorum satırları, README ve commit mesajları yazma rehberi. Teknik terimlerin Türkçe karşılıkları ve Türk geliştirici topluluğu standartları.
skill.summary: Write consistent Turkish comments, README files, commit messages, and technical terminology for software projects.
skill.author: cesareth

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found Markdown code fences, inline examples, and ordinary text, with no executable scripts, network access, credential access, or prompt injection attempts. The skill is safe to publish with low residual risk from documented shell examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown code fences, inline code terms, and sample README or commit instructions. They do not execute commands or construct shell input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are inside documentation sections and fenced examples. No script file, automation hook, or runtime command execution path was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detector Matched Ordinary Text
low_findings.description: The high severity weak cryptography hits are not cryptographic APIs. They match the description text, a Turkish character note, and a file size example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural language and format examples, not hashing or encryption calls. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or related weak algorithms being used.

content.value_statement: Turkish software teams need consistent wording for comments, README files, commit messages, and technical terms. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Turkish documentation standards.
actual_capabilities: Writes Turkish comment and docstring guidance for Python, TypeScript, and Go examples.
Provides Turkish naming guidance for domain variables and framework terms.
Defines a Turkish commit message format with approved change type labels.
Supplies a README structure for setup, quick start, features, configuration, contribution, and license sections.
Improves Turkish error messages, log messages, and technical term choices.
limitations: Does not inspect a full repository unless the user provides files or context.
Does not enforce documentation rules automatically in CI or editor tooling.
Does not replace project-specific glossary decisions made by a team.
May keep common English framework terms when Turkish wording would reduce clarity.

use_cases.title: Standardize Turkish Project Documentation
use_cases.description: Create consistent README sections, setup text, and contribution notes for a Turkish software project.

use_cases.title: Improve Code Comments Before Review
use_cases.description: Rewrite unclear comments and docstrings so they explain intent in clear Turkish.

use_cases.title: Create Team Terminology Rules
use_cases.description: Choose Turkish equivalents for common software terms and decide which framework terms stay in English.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Comment Rewrite
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for one function or short file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite these comments in clear Turkish. Keep technical terms consistent and explain why the code exists.

prompt_templates.title: README Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a starter README for a Turkish project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Turkish README for this project. Include purpose, setup, quick start, features, configuration, contribution, and license sections.

prompt_templates.title: Commit Message Cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a clean commit history for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these changes into Turkish commit messages. Use the approved type labels and keep the subject under 72 characters.

prompt_templates.title: Documentation Consistency Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across comments, messages, and terms.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Turkish project documentation for consistency. Flag mixed language, unclear terms, weak comments, and missing README sections.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a Turkish commit message for adding profile photo uploads.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests a concise Turkish subject with an approved type label, plus an optional detail explaining upload limits.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks whether to translate request, response, and callback.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends keeping common framework terms in English while translating business domain names consistently.

output_examples.input: A team asks for a README outline for a new package.
output_examples.output: The skill returns Turkish sections for purpose, installation, quick start, features, configuration, contribution, and license.

best_practices: Provide the target audience and project type before asking for documentation.
Share existing naming rules so the skill can keep terms consistent.
Ask for review notes when updating an established project glossary.
anti_patterns: Do not translate framework keywords when it makes code harder to understand.
Do not mix Turkish and English domain terms without a clear rule.
Do not use comments to repeat obvious code behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill write code?
faq.answer: It focuses on Turkish documentation, comments, README text, commit messages, and terminology guidance.

faq.question: Can it review existing Turkish comments?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the comments or files, and ask for clarity, consistency, and terminology review.

faq.question: Does it support English-only projects?
faq.answer: It is designed for Turkish or bilingual projects. English-only projects may need a different documentation style.

faq.question: Can it create commit messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Turkish change labels such as ekle, düzelt, güncelle, test, and belge.

faq.question: Does it include a terminology glossary?
faq.answer: Yes. It references Turkish equivalents for common software, database, API, DevOps, security, and AI terms.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Manual audit found only Markdown examples and ordinary documentation text. No executable behavior or data access was found.
